metadata.title: Why a Dataset With Zero Missing Values Can Still Be Missing Your Data
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Reallocation Engine candidate 04. Source chapter: books/the-reallocation-engine/chapters/05-verifying-the-data.md (the join-fragmentation / name-matching mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, dusty navy = matched/joined/counted, crimson = orphaned/dropped/uncounted, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: dusty navy #3D5A80 = matched / joined / counted; crimson #BF3339 = orphaned / dropped / uncounted (the comma is crimson — the villain); never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO six-step interrogation procedure, NO normalization code, NO fuzzy-matching fixes, NO base-rate / Bayes material (that is candidate 05 — this film never shows the 0.68 -> 0.38 correction or the 8% prior), NO verb taxonomy. One mechanism only: a punctuation-blind join fragments one company into buckets that never meet, so the missingness lives BETWEEN the two datasets where no within-table check can see it. Worked figures taken verbatim from the chapter's BioTechCo trace (12 filings = 10 'BioTechCo LLC' + 2 'BioTechCo, LLC'; USCIS 'BIOTECHCO LLC'; join normalizes case but not punctuation; count reads 10 not 12; missingness check reports zero missing).

beats.narration_text: The audit runs clean. Zero missing values, every row real, every field filled. And the count it hands you is still quietly, confidently wrong.

card.copy: Why a dataset with zero missing values can still be missing your data
card.sub: the gap lives between two tables

beats.narration_text: Here's the report. Missing values: zero. A green check. It isn't lying to you. It genuinely cannot see what went wrong.
beats.scene_description: a printed data-audit report pinned to the newsprint ground — a clean summary table with a prominent 'Missing values: 0' row and a green checkmark; slow push-in onto the zero and the check
beats.image_prompt: printed screenshot of a tidy data-quality audit report, a summary table with a highlighted row reading 'Missing values: 0' beside a green checkmark, pinned like a clipping to aged newsprint, desaturated flat print reproduction, editorial collage
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] the clean audit — push-in on the green zero

beats.narration_text: Because to that check, missing means one thing: an empty cell. A blank. It looks down every column for holes, and finds none.

document.attribution: — all the missingness check actually verifies

beats.scene_description: the checker's whole definition of 'missing' as a large serif quote card; gold highlighter sweeps 'full' as it is spoken

beats.narration_text: But here's what it's checking. Two separate datasets. Labor Department filings on one side, immigration approvals on the other. You join them on the company name.

production_viz.label: two tables, joined on name
production_viz.mechanic: two stacked-row tables slide in from left and right, each a short column of navy row-bars with a company-name chip; a serif label 'join on name' sits in the gap between them
production_viz.note: no counts yet — just establish the two sources and the seam between them

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two tables face each other across a seam

beats.narration_text: Match the names, and a company's records line up across both sides. Rows that agree flow through the gate and join. That's the whole mechanism.

production_viz.label: names match → rows join
production_viz.mechanic: rows from both tables converge on a central name-matching gate; matched pairs pass through and merge into a single navy 'joined' bucket on the right
production_viz.note: the gate is the through-line object for B05–B10

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] rows pass the gate, merge into the joined bucket

beats.narration_text: Now take one real company. Twelve filings over three years. Except the name got typed two ways — ten as BioTechCo LLC, and two with a comma.
beats.scene_description: one company's twelve filing rows stacked; ten carry the name chip 'BioTechCo LLC', two carry 'BioTechCo, LLC' with the comma set in crimson; a bracket labels all twelve 'same legal filer'

production_viz.label: 12 filings · one filer · two spellings
production_viz.mechanic: 12 row-bars stack; 10 tagged 'BioTechCo LLC' in navy, 2 tagged 'BioTechCo, LLC' with a crimson comma; a hand bracket + serif label 'same legal filer' spans all twelve
production_viz.note: figures verbatim from the chapter's worked trace — 12 = 10 + 2

viz.label: one company, twelve filings, two name variants

beats.narration_text: The join folded away capital letters. But not punctuation. So one stray comma sends those two rows straight past the gate, into a bucket that never joins.

production_viz.label: one comma → the split
production_viz.mechanic: the twelve rows advance toward the gate; a giant crimson comma drops in at the gate mouth and deflects the 2 'BioTechCo, LLC' rows sideways into a crimson 'orphan' bucket while the 10 pass through to the navy joined bucket — the SPLIT move
production_viz.note: manim move: split. The comma is the villain — crimson, oversized.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the giant comma deflects 2 rows into the orphan bucket

beats.narration_text: Now the count reads ten, not twelve. The approval rate is computed on the wrong denominator. Every record is correct. The total is not.
beats.scene_description: the navy joined bucket labels itself '10' in large serif; a struck-through '12' sits faded above it; the crimson orphan bucket sits apart, still holding its 2 rows; annotation label 'wrong denominator'

production_viz.label: count = 10, not 12
production_viz.mechanic: the joined bucket resolves to a big navy '10'; a faded '12' with a thin strike sits above; the crimson orphan bucket holds '2' off to the side; serif label 'wrong denominator' under the ten
production_viz.note: no approval-rate number printed — the point is the denominator, not a Bayes update

viz.label: the total is wrong though every row is right

beats.narration_text: And the missingness check? It scans down each column, inside each table, and reports zero missing. Every row it can see really is right there.

production_viz.label: the check scans within — finds nothing
production_viz.mechanic: a magnifying glass sweeps down the columns of BOTH tables in turn; each cell it passes ticks navy; it lands on a green 'MISSING: 0' chip — while the crimson orphan bucket sits outside its path, never scanned
production_viz.note: the reveal — the check is honest and blind at once

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the magnifier scans within-table, misses the orphan

beats.narration_text: Because the gap isn't in either table. It lives between them, in the join. And no check that looks inside one table can ever look there.
beats.scene_description: both tables and the orphan bucket sit on screen; the editor's pen draws one crimson ring around the SEAM between the two tables — the empty space where the orphan fell — timed to 'between'

production_viz.mechanic: end-state with both tables + crimson orphan bucket; a HandRing lands around the gap BETWEEN the tables on the word 'between'
production_viz.note: one ring only (restraint rule) — the ring targets empty space, not a mobject

annotation.target: the seam between the two tables

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] ring around the gap between the tables

beats.narration_text: Zero missing doesn't mean nothing's missing. It means nothing's missing where you looked.
beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — nothing missing where you looked
